intro:
The Jordan (Hebrew Yarden, "the descender," from its steep fall) is the chief river of the Holy Land. Fed by the snows of Mount Hermon, it flows south through the Sea of Galilee and down the deep Jordan Rift Valley to empty into the Dead Sea — a winding course of some two hundred miles that descends well below sea level, the lowest river on earth. Through much of Israel's history it marked the eastern boundary of Canaan proper, dividing the land of promise from the territory beyond, and its annual springtime flood (Joshua 3:15) made its crossing a memorable act of faith.

The river threads through the Old Testament story. Lot, choosing by sight, took the well-watered "plain of the Jordan" toward Sodom (Genesis 13:10–11), and Jacob recalled crossing it with only his staff (Genesis 32:10). Its supreme moment came when Israel entered the promised land: as the priests bearing the ark stepped into the flood, the waters were cut off and "stood in a heap," and the nation passed over on dry ground, raising twelve memorial stones at Gilgal (Joshua 3:14–17; Joshua 4:20–24) — an echo of the Red Sea remembered in worship: "the Jordan turned back" (Psalm 114:3; 5). As a frontier it occasioned the altar of witness built by the eastern tribes (Joshua 22:10–11), and its fords were strategic in the days of the judges (Judges 3:28; Judges 12:5–6).

The river is woven into the prophetic narratives of Elijah and Elisha. Elijah parted it with his rolled cloak and was taken up to heaven beyond it, whereupon Elisha received a double portion of his spirit and struck the waters to part them again (2 Kings 2:8, 13–14); there Elisha made an axehead float (2 Kings 6:6). Most famously, the Syrian commander Naaman, told to wash seven times in the Jordan, was cleansed of his leprosy when at last he humbled himself to obey — a sign that the God of Israel shows mercy even to a Gentile (2 Kings 5:10–14).

In the New Testament the Jordan becomes the scene of new beginnings. John the Baptist preached and baptized "in the Jordan" those who confessed their sins (Matthew 3:5–6), and there Jesus himself was baptized, the Spirit descending as a dove and the Father's voice declaring him the beloved Son (Mark 1:9–11); to its eastern bank he later withdrew (John 10:40). So the river that bounded the inheritance gained a lasting place in Christian devotion: Israel's crossing into Canaan is read as a type of passing through death into the promised rest, and the waters of the Jordan as a fitting emblem of the baptism by which believers die and rise with Christ. Its very name, traditionally rendered "river of judgment," came to evoke that passage from the old life to the new.
